questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the net energy absorbed by an atom during a process in which it absorbs a photon of wavelength $500 \text{ nm}$ and subsequently emits another photon of wavelength $700 \text{ nm}$.
questions.solution:
The net energy absorbed by the atom is the difference between the energy of the absorbed photon and the energy of the emitted photon.

$\Delta E = E_{\text{absorbed}} - E_{\text{emitted}}$

$\Delta E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\text{absorbed}}} - \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_{\text{emitted}}}$

Substituting the given values, $\lambda_{\text{absorbed}} = 500 \text{ nm}$ and $\lambda_{\text{emitted}} = 700 \text{ nm}$:

$\Delta E = hc \left( \dfrac{1}{500 \times 10^{-9}} - \dfrac{1}{700 \times 10^{-9}} \right)$

Using $hc = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8 = 19.89 \times 10^{-26} \text{ J m}$:

$\Delta E = 19.89 \times 10^{-26} \times 10^9 \left( \dfrac{1}{500} - \dfrac{1}{700} \right)$

$\Delta E = 19.89 \times 10^{-17} \left( \dfrac{7 - 5}{3500} \right)$

$\Delta E = 19.89 \times 10^{-17} \times \dfrac{2}{3500}$

$\Delta E = \dfrac{39.78 \times 10^{-17}}{3500} \approx 1.136 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Rounding to two significant figures, the net energy absorbed is $1.1 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Compute the number of photons emitted per second by a $10 \text{ W}$ sodium vapour lamp. Assume that $60\%$ of the consumed energy is converted into light. The wavelength of sodium light is $590 \text{ nm}$.
questions.solution:
Power of the lamp, $P = 10 \text{ W}$

Energy converted into light per second, $E_{\text{light}} = \dfrac{60}{100} \times 10 = 6 \text{ J/s}$

Wavelength of sodium light, $\lambda = 590 \text{ nm} = 590 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$

Energy of one photon is given by $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

$E = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{590 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 3.37 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Number of photons emitted per second, $n = \dfrac{E_{\text{light}}}{E}$

$n = \dfrac{6}{3.37 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 1.77 \times 10^{19}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assuming the sun is directly overhead, the surface of the earth receives sunlight at $1.4 	imes 10^3 \text{ W m}^{-2}$. Treat the light as monochromatic with an average wavelength of $500 \text{ nm}$, and assume no light is absorbed between the sun and the earth's surface. The distance from the sun to the earth is $1.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$. Determine the number of photons falling per second on each square metre of the earth's surface directly below the sun.
questions.solution:
Intensity of sunlight on the earth's surface, $I = 1.4 \times 10^3 \text{ W m}^{-2}$

Energy of a single photon is given by $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

Substituting the given values:

$E = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{500 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.98 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

The number of photons falling per second per square metre is $n = \dfrac{I}{E}$

$n = \dfrac{1.4 \times 10^3}{3.98 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 3.5 \times 10^{21}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assuming the sun is directly overhead, the surface of the earth receives sunlight at $1.4 \times 10^3 \text{ W m}^{-2}$. Treat the light as monochromatic with an average wavelength of $500 \text{ nm}$, and assume no light is absorbed between the sun and the earth's surface. The distance from the sun to the earth is $1.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$. How many photons are present in each cubic metre near the earth's surface at any instant?
questions.solution:
The intensity of sunlight at the earth's surface is given by $I = 1.4 \times 10^3 \text{ W m}^{-2}$.

The energy of a single photon is given by $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.

The number of photons crossing a unit area per unit time is $n = \dfrac{I}{E} = \dfrac{I \lambda}{hc}$.

Since these photons travel at the speed of light $c$, the number of photons present per unit volume (photon density) is $N = \dfrac{n}{c} = \dfrac{I \lambda}{hc^2}$.

Substituting the given values:

$N = \dfrac{1.4 \times 10^3 \times 500 \times 10^{-9}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times (3 \times 10^8)^2}$

$N = \dfrac{7 \times 10^{-4}}{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 9 \times 10^{16}}$

$N = \dfrac{7 \times 10^{-4}}{59.67 \times 10^{-18}}$

$N \approx 1.17 \times 10^{13} \text{ m}^{-3}$

Rounding to appropriate significant figures, the number of photons per cubic metre is approximately $1.2 \times 10^{13}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Assuming the sun is directly overhead, the surface of the earth receives sunlight at $1.4 \times 10^3 \text{ W m}^{-2}$. Treat the light as monochromatic with an average wavelength of $500 \text{ nm}$, and assume no light is absorbed between the sun and the earth's surface. The distance from the sun to the earth is $1.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$. Determine the number of photons the sun emits per second.
questions.solution:
Intensity of sunlight at the earth's surface, $I = 1.4 \times 10^3 \text{ W m}^{-2}$

Distance from the sun to the earth, $R = 1.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}$

Total power emitted by the sun, $P = I \times 4 \pi R^2$

$P = 1.4 \times 10^3 \times 4 \times 3.14 \times (1.5 \times 10^{11})^2 = 3.96 \times 10^{26} \text{ W}$

Energy of a single photon, $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

$E = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{500 \times 10^{-9}} = 3.98 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Number of photons emitted per second, $N = \dfrac{P}{E}$

$N = \dfrac{3.96 \times 10^{26}}{3.98 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 9.9 \times 10^{44}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the momentum of a light photon that has a wavelength of $500 \text{ nm}$.
questions.solution:
The momentum of a photon is given by the de Broglie relation:

$p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$

Given $\lambda = 500 \text{ nm} = 500 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$ and Planck's constant $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$.

Substituting the values:

$p = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{500 \times 10^{-9}}$

$p = \dfrac{6.63}{5} \times 10^{-27}$

$p = 1.326 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg m/s}$

Rounding off, we get $1.33 \times 10^{-27} \text{ kg m/s}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Visible light encompasses wavelengths ranging from $400 \text{ nm}$ to $780 \text{ nm}$. Determine the energy range of the photons of visible light.
questions.solution:
The energy of a photon is given by the formula $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.

For the lower limit of wavelength ($\lambda = 400 \text{ nm}$):
$E_{\text{max}} = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{400 \times 10^{-9}}$
$E_{\text{max}} = 4.97 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \approx 5.00 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

For the upper limit of wavelength ($\lambda = 780 \text{ nm}$):
$E_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{6.626 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{780 \times 10^{-9}}$
$E_{\text{min}} = 2.55 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} \approx 2.56 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Thus, the energy range of the photons of visible light is $2.56 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$ to $5.00 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A beam of white light strikes a plane surface normally. The surface absorbs $70\%$ of the light while reflecting the remainder. Determine the force exerted on the surface by the beam if it carries a power of $10 \text{ W}$.
questions.solution:
The force exerted by a light beam on a surface is given by the rate of change of momentum of the photons.

For a beam of power $P$ incident normally, the initial momentum incident per second is $\dfrac{P}{c}$.

The surface absorbs $70\%$ and reflects $30\%$ of the light.

The force due to the absorbed light is $F_{a} = \dfrac{0.7 P}{c}$.

The force due to the reflected light is $F_{r} = \dfrac{2 \times 0.3 P}{c} = \dfrac{0.6 P}{c}$.

The total force exerted on the surface is $F = F_{a} + F_{r} = \dfrac{1.3 P}{c}$.

Substituting $P = 10 \text{ W}$ and $c = 3 \times 10^{8} \text{ m/s}$:

$F = \dfrac{1.3 \times 10}{3 \times 10^{8}} = \dfrac{13}{3} \times 10^{-8} \text{ N}$

$F \approx 4.33 \times 10^{-8} \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small, totally reflecting plane mirror is positioned horizontally to face a parallel beam of light, as shown in the figure. The mirror has a mass of $20 \text{ g}$. Assuming no absorption occurs in the lens and that $30\%$ of the light emitted by the source passes through the lens, determine the power of the source required to support the weight of the mirror. Take $g = 10 \text{ m s}^{-2}$.
questions.solution:
Let $P$ be the power of the source. The power of the light that passes through the lens and is incident on the mirror is $P' = 0.30 P$.

Since the mirror is totally reflecting, the force exerted by the light beam on the mirror is given by the rate of change of momentum of the photons:

$F = \dfrac{2P'}{c} = \dfrac{2(0.30 P)}{c} = \dfrac{0.60 P}{c}$

For the light to support the weight of the mirror, this force must be equal to the gravitational force acting on the mirror:

$F = mg$

Substituting the given values ($m = 20 \text{ g} = 0.02 \text{ kg}$, $g = 10 \text{ m s}^{-2}$, and $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$):

$\dfrac{0.60 P}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.02 \times 10$

$\dfrac{0.60 P}{3 \times 10^8} = 0.2$

$0.60 P = 0.2 \times 3 \times 10^8$

$0.60 P = 0.6 \times 10^8$

$P = 1.0 \times 10^8 \text{ W}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A $100 \text{ W}$ light bulb is positioned at the centre of a spherical chamber having a radius of $20 \text{ cm}$. Assuming that $60\%$ of the energy supplied to the bulb is converted into light and the chamber's surface is perfectly absorbing, determine the pressure exerted by the light on the surface of the chamber.
questions.solution:
Power of the light emitted, $P = 60\% \text{ of } 100 \text{ W} = 60 \text{ W}$

Radius of the spherical chamber, $r = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$

Intensity of light at the surface of the chamber is given by:

$I = \dfrac{P}{4 \pi r^2}$

For a perfectly absorbing surface, the radiation pressure $p$ is:

$p = \dfrac{I}{c} = \dfrac{P}{4 \pi r^2 c}$

Substituting the given values:

$p = \dfrac{60}{4 \pi (0.2)^2 \times 3 \times 10^8}$

$p = \dfrac{60}{4 \pi \times 0.04 \times 3 \times 10^8}$

$p = \dfrac{60}{0.48 \pi \times 10^8}$

$p = \dfrac{125}{\pi} \times 10^{-8} \text{ Pa}$

$p \approx 39.8 \times 10^{-8} \text{ Pa} = 3.98 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Pa} \approx 4.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ Pa}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sphere of radius $1.00 \text{ cm}$ is positioned in the path of a parallel beam of light with a large aperture. The light has an intensity of $0.50 \text{ W cm}^{-2}$. Assuming the sphere completely absorbs the incident radiation, determine the force applied by the light beam on the sphere.
questions.solution:
The effective area of the sphere intercepting the parallel beam of light is its projected cross-sectional area, $A = \pi r^2$.

Given $r = 1.00 \text{ cm}$, the area is $A = \pi (1.00)^2 = \pi \text{ cm}^2$.

The total power absorbed by the sphere is $P = I A$.

Substituting $I = 0.50 \text{ W cm}^{-2}$, we get $P = 0.50 \times \pi = 0.50 \pi \text{ W}$.

Since the sphere completely absorbs the incident radiation, the force exerted by the light beam is the momentum transferred per unit time, $F = \dfrac{P}{c}$.

Using $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m s}^{-1}$:

$F = \dfrac{0.50 \pi}{3 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{1.57}{3 \times 10^8} \approx 5.2 \times 10^{-9} \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Why is it not possible for a photon to be completely absorbed by a free electron?
options: The electron would need to acquire a velocity greater than the speed of light.
The photon cannot transfer its entire energy without also transferring its rest mass.
Simultaneous conservation of both energy and linear momentum cannot be achieved.
A free electron lacks the discrete energy levels required for photon absorption.
questions.solution:
Assume a photon of frequency $ u$ is completely absorbed by a free electron initially at rest.

Let the rest mass of the electron be $m_0$ and its final velocity be $v$.

By conservation of energy:
$h u + m_0 c^2 = mc^2$

By conservation of linear momentum:
$\dfrac{h u}{c} = mv \Rightarrow h u = mvc$

Substituting $h u$ from the momentum equation into the energy equation:
$mvc + m_0 c^2 = mc^2$
$m_0 c^2 = mc(c - v)$

Using the relativistic mass formula $m = \dfrac{m_0}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}}$:
$m_0 c^2 = \dfrac{m_0 c^2 \left(1 - \dfrac{v}{c} ight)}{\sqrt{1 - \dfrac{v^2}{c^2}}}$

$1 = \sqrt{\dfrac{1 - \dfrac{v}{c}}{1 + \dfrac{v}{c}}}$

Squaring both sides:
$1 + \dfrac{v}{c} = 1 - \dfrac{v}{c} \Rightarrow \dfrac{2v}{c} = 0 \Rightarrow v = 0$

If $v = 0$, then the photon energy $h u = mvc = 0$, which implies no photon was present. Thus, simultaneous conservation of energy and linear momentum cannot be achieved.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A sphere of radius $1.00 \text{ cm}$ is positioned in the path of a parallel beam of light of large aperture. The intensity of the light is $0.50 \text{ W cm}^{-2}$. If the sphere is not perfectly absorbing, how does the force on the sphere due to the light falling on it compare to the case where it completely absorbs the radiation?
options: The force is greater than when it completely absorbs the radiation
The force is the same as when it completely absorbs the radiation
The force depends on the specific absorption coefficient of the sphere
The force is less than when it completely absorbs the radiation
questions.solution:
Let the intensity of the incident light be $I$ and the radius of the sphere be $R$.

For a perfectly absorbing sphere, the light is completely absorbed by the projected cross-sectional area $\pi R^2$. The force exerted is the rate of change of momentum:
$F_{abs} = \dfrac{I}{c} \pi R^2$

For a perfectly reflecting sphere, consider an elemental ring on the surface of the sphere at an angle $\theta$ with respect to the direction of the incident beam. The area of this element is $dS = 2\pi R^2 \sin \theta d\theta$.

The power incident on this elemental area is $P = I \cos \theta dS$.
Since the light is specularly reflected, the change in momentum per unit time (force) is directed along the normal to the surface:
$dF = \dfrac{2P}{c} \cos \theta = \dfrac{2I}{c} \cos^2 \theta dS$

The component of this force along the direction of the incident beam (z-axis) is:
$dF_z = dF \cos \theta = \dfrac{2I}{c} \cos^3 \theta dS$

Substituting $dS = 2\pi R^2 \sin \theta d\theta$, we get:
$dF_z = \dfrac{4\pi R^2 I}{c} \cos^3 \theta \sin \theta d\theta$

Integrating this over the illuminated hemisphere (from $\theta = 0$ to $\theta = \pi/2$):
$F_{ref} = \dfrac{4\pi R^2 I}{c} \int_0^{\pi/2} \cos^3 \theta \sin \theta d\theta$

Let $\cos \theta = t$, then $-\sin \theta d\theta = dt$. The limits change from $1$ to $0$:
$F_{ref} = \dfrac{4\pi R^2 I}{c} \int_0^1 t^3 dt = \dfrac{4\pi R^2 I}{c} \left[ \dfrac{t^4}{4} \right]_0^1 = \dfrac{\pi R^2 I}{c}$

We can see that $F_{ref} = F_{abs}$. The force on a perfectly reflecting sphere is exactly equal to the force on a perfectly absorbing sphere.

Since any opaque partially absorbing sphere can be considered as a combination of an absorbing fraction $\alpha$ and a reflecting fraction $\rho$ (where $\alpha + \rho = 1$), the total force will be:
$F = \alpha F_{abs} + \rho F_{ref} = (\alpha + \rho) \dfrac{\pi R^2 I}{c} = \dfrac{\pi R^2 I}{c}$

Therefore, the force on the sphere is the same as when it completely absorbs the radiation.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted when light of wavelength $350 \text{ nm}$ strikes a cesium surface. The work function of cesium is $1.9 \text{ eV}$.
questions.solution:
The energy of the incident photon is given by $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

Using $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV nm}$ and $\lambda = 350 \text{ nm}$:

$E = \dfrac{1240}{350} \approx 3.54 \text{ eV}$

According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is:

$K_{\text{max}} = E - \Phi$

Substituting the values of $E$ and the work function $\Phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$:

$K_{\text{max}} = 3.54 \text{ eV} - 1.9 \text{ eV} = 1.64 \text{ eV}$

The closest value among the given options is $1.6 \text{ eV}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Placed $1 \text{ m}$ apart, two initially neutral particles undergo a process where charge is transferred from one particle to the other. Assuming the lost electric potential energy is entirely transformed into a photon, determine the longest and the next smaller possible wavelengths of the photon.
questions.solution:
When charge is transferred between two initially neutral particles, one acquires a charge $+q$ and the other $-q$. The charge must be an integer multiple of the elementary charge, so $q = ne$ where $n = 1, 2, 3, \dots$

The initial electric potential energy of the system is zero. The final electric potential energy is:

$U_f = -\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0}\dfrac{q^2}{r} = -\dfrac{n^2 e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r}$

The lost electric potential energy is converted into a photon of wavelength $\lambda$. Thus, we have:

$\Delta U = 0 - U_f = \dfrac{n^2 e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r}$

Equating this to the energy of the photon, $\dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$:

$\dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{n^2 e^2}{4\pi\epsilon_0 r} \Rightarrow \lambda = \dfrac{4\pi\epsilon_0 h c r}{n^2 e^2}$

The longest wavelength corresponds to the minimum energy, which occurs for $n = 1$. Substituting the standard values $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$, $c = 3 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s}$, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, $r = 1 \text{ m}$, and $\dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} = 9 \times 10^9 \text{ N m}^2/\text{C}^2$:

$\lambda_1 = \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{9 \times 10^9 \times (1.6 \times 10^{-19})^2}$

$\lambda_1 = \dfrac{19.8 \times 10^{-26}}{23.04 \times 10^{-29}} \approx 860 \text{ m}$

The next smaller wavelength corresponds to $n = 2$:

$\lambda_2 = \dfrac{\lambda_1}{2^2} = \dfrac{860}{4} = 215 \text{ m}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal has a work function of $2.5 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$. Determine the threshold frequency for photoelectric emission.
questions.solution:
The work function of a metal is related to its threshold frequency by the equation $\Phi = h u_0$, where $h$ is Planck's constant and $ u_0$ is the threshold frequency.

Substituting the given values:

$ u_0 = \dfrac{\Phi}{h}$

$ u_0 = \dfrac{2.5 imes 10^{-19}}{6.63 imes 10^{-34}}$

$ u_0 \approx 3.77 imes 10^{14} ext{ Hz}$

Rounding to one decimal place, $ u_0 \approx 3.8 imes 10^{14} ext{ Hz}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal has a work function of $2.5 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$. When the metal is illuminated by a light beam of frequency $6.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$, what is the stopping potential?
questions.solution:
The energy of the incident photon is given by $E = h\nu$.

Taking Planck's constant $h = 6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$:

$E = (6.6 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}) \times (6.0 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}) = 3.96 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is:

$K_{\text{max}} = E - \Phi$

$K_{\text{max}} = 3.96 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} - 2.5 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 1.46 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

The stopping potential $V_0$ is related to the maximum kinetic energy by $K_{\text{max}} = eV_0$.

$V_0 = \dfrac{K_{\text{max}}}{e} = \dfrac{1.46 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}} = 0.9125 \text{ V}$

Rounding off, we get $V_0 \approx 0.91 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photoelectric material has a work function of $4.0 \text{ eV}$. Determine its threshold wavelength.
questions.solution:
The work function $\Phi$ and threshold wavelength $\lambda_0$ are related by the equation:

$\Phi = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda_0}$

Given $\Phi = 4.0 \text{ eV}$ and using the standard value $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}$, we get:

$\lambda_0 = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}}{4.0 \text{ eV}}$

$\lambda_0 = 310 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a photoelectric material with a work function of $4.0 \text{ eV}$, calculate the wavelength of incident light that results in a stopping potential of $2.5 \text{ V}$.
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$E = \Phi + K_{\max}$

Given work function $\Phi = 4.0 \text{ eV}$ and stopping potential $V_0 = 2.5 \text{ V}$.

The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is $K_{\max} = eV_0 = 2.5 \text{ eV}$.

The energy of the incident photon is:

$E = 4.0 \text{ eV} + 2.5 \text{ eV} = 6.5 \text{ eV}$

The wavelength of the incident light is given by $\lambda = \dfrac{hc}{E}$.

Using $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{1240}{6.5} \approx 191 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal plate is illuminated by a monochromatic beam of light with a wavelength of $400 \text{ nm}$, and a negative potential of $1.1 \text{ V}$ is required to halt the photocurrent. Determine the threshold wavelength for the metal.
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$E = \Phi + K_{\text{max}}$

The energy of the incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}}{400 \text{ nm}} = 3.1 \text{ eV}$

The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons is related to the stopping potential $V_0$ as:

$K_{\text{max}} = eV_0 = 1.1 \text{ eV}$

Substituting these values into the photoelectric equation:

$\Phi = E - K_{\text{max}} = 3.1 \text{ eV} - 1.1 \text{ eV} = 2.0 \text{ eV}$

The threshold wavelength $\lambda_0$ is given by:

$\lambda_0 = \dfrac{hc}{\Phi} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}}{2.0 \text{ eV}} = 620 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In a photoelectric effect experiment, the stopping potential is measured for monochromatic light beams of varying wavelengths. The collected data is as follows:
wavelength (nm)$350$$400$$450$$500$$550$
stopping potential (V)$1.45$$1.00$$0.66$$0.38$$0.16$

Determine the Planck constant from this data.
questions.solution:
Einstein's photoelectric equation is given by:

$eV_s = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \Phi$

$V_s = \dfrac{hc}{e} \left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\right) - \dfrac{\Phi}{e}$

The slope of the $V_s$ versus $\dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ graph is $m = \dfrac{hc}{e}$.

Taking the data points $\lambda_1 = 350 \text{ nm}, V_{s1} = 1.45 \text{ V}$ and $\lambda_2 = 400 \text{ nm}, V_{s2} = 1.00 \text{ V}$:

$m = \dfrac{\Delta V_s}{\Delta \left(\dfrac{1}{\lambda}\right)} = \dfrac{1.45 - 1.00}{\dfrac{1}{350} - \dfrac{1}{400}} \text{ V nm}$

$m = \dfrac{0.45}{\dfrac{50}{140000}} = 0.45 \times 2800 = 1260 \text{ V nm}$

Equating this to $\dfrac{hc}{e}$:

$\dfrac{hc}{e} = 1260 \times 10^{-9} \text{ V m}$

$h = \dfrac{1260 \times 10^{-9} \text{ eV m}}{c} = \dfrac{1260 \times 10^{-9}}{3 \times 10^8} \text{ eV s}$

$h = 420 \times 10^{-17} \text{ eV s} = 4.2 \times 10^{-15} \text{ eV s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A parallel beam of monochromatic light having a wavelength of $663 \text{ nm}$ is incident on a totally reflecting plane mirror. The angle of incidence is $60^\circ$, and $1.0 \times 10^{19}$ photons strike the mirror each second. Determine the force exerted by the light beam on the mirror.
questions.solution:
The momentum of a single photon is given by $p = \dfrac{h}{\lambda}$.

When a photon strikes a totally reflecting mirror at an angle of incidence $\theta$, the component of its momentum perpendicular to the mirror is $p \cos \theta$. Upon reflection, this perpendicular component reverses its direction, while the parallel component remains unchanged.

The change in momentum of each photon is:
$\Delta p = 2p \cos \theta = \dfrac{2h}{\lambda} \cos \theta$

The force exerted on the mirror is the total change in momentum per second, which is the product of the number of photons striking per second ($n$) and the change in momentum of each photon:
$F = n \Delta p = \dfrac{2nh}{\lambda} \cos \theta$

Substituting the given values ($n = 1.0 \times 10^{19} \text{ s}^{-1}$, $h = 6.63 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J s}$, $\lambda = 663 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}$, $\theta = 60^\circ$):
$F = \dfrac{2 \times 1.0 \times 10^{19} \times 6.63 \times 10^{-34}}{663 \times 10^{-9}} \times \cos 60^\circ$

$F = 2 \times 10^{19} \times 10^{-27} \times \dfrac{1}{2}$

$F = 1.0 \times 10^{-8} \text{ N}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In a photoelectric effect experiment, the stopping potential is measured for monochromatic light beams of varying wavelengths. Find the threshold wavelength from the experimental data.
wavelength (nm)$350$$400$$450$$500$$550$
stopping potential (V)$1.45$$1.00$$0.66$$0.38$$0.16$
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$V_s = \dfrac{hc}{e\lambda} - \Phi$

Using the data points $\lambda_1 = 350 \text{ nm}$, $V_{s1} = 1.45 \text{ V}$ and $\lambda_2 = 400 \text{ nm}$, $V_{s2} = 1.00 \text{ V}$:

$1.45 = \dfrac{hc}{350e} - \Phi$

$1.00 = \dfrac{hc}{400e} - \Phi$

Subtracting the second equation from the first:

$0.45 = \dfrac{hc}{e} \left( \dfrac{1}{350} - \dfrac{1}{400} \right)$

$0.45 = \dfrac{hc}{e} \left( \dfrac{400 - 350}{350 \times 400} \right) = \dfrac{hc}{e} \left( \dfrac{50}{140000} \right)$

$\dfrac{hc}{e} = 0.45 \times 2800 = 1260 \text{ V nm}$

Substituting this value back into the second equation to find the work function $\Phi$:

$1.00 = \dfrac{1260}{400} - \Phi$

$\Phi = 3.15 - 1.00 = 2.15 \text{ eV}$

The threshold wavelength $\lambda_0$ is given by:

$\lambda_0 = \dfrac{hc}{e\Phi}$

$\lambda_0 = \dfrac{1260}{2.15} \approx 586 \text{ nm}$

This value is closest to $585 \text{ nm}$.

Answer: $585 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For a monochromatic beam, the associated electric field becomes zero $1.2 \times 10^{15}$ times per second. Determine the maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons emitted when this light is incident on a metal surface having a work function of $2.0 \text{ eV}$.
questions.solution:
The electric field of an electromagnetic wave becomes zero twice in each cycle.

Frequency of the light, $\nu = \dfrac{1.2 \times 10^{15}}{2} = 6 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$

Energy of the incident photon, $E = h\nu$

$E = (4.14 \times 10^{-15} \text{ eV s}) \times (6 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}) = 2.484 \text{ eV}$

Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is given by:

$K_{\max} = E - \Phi$

$K_{\max} = 2.484 \text{ eV} - 2.0 \text{ eV} = 0.484 \text{ eV} \approx 0.48 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A light wave has an associated electric field given by $E = E_0 \sin\!\left[(1.57 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1})(x - ct)\right]$. Determine the stopping potential if this light is utilized in a photoelectric effect experiment where the emitter possesses a work function of $1.9 \text{ eV}$.
questions.solution:
The given electric field is $E = E_0 \sin[(1.57 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1})(x - ct)]$.

Comparing this with the standard wave equation $E = E_0 \sin[k(x - ct)]$, the wave number is $k = 1.57 \times 10^7 \text{ m}^{-1}$.

Since $k = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}$, the wavelength $\lambda$ is:

$\lambda = \dfrac{2\pi}{1.57 \times 10^7} = \dfrac{2 \times 3.14}{1.57 \times 10^7} = 4 \times 10^{-7} \text{ m} = 4000 \text{ \AA}$

The energy of the incident photon is:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{12400 \text{ eV \AA}}{4000 \text{ \AA}} = 3.1 \text{ eV}$

From Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy $K_{\max}$ is:

$K_{\max} = E - \Phi$

Substituting the given work function $\Phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$:

$K_{\max} = 3.1 \text{ eV} - 1.9 \text{ eV} = 1.2 \text{ eV}$

The stopping potential $V_0$ is given by $eV_0 = K_{\max}$, which yields:

$V_0 = 1.2 \text{ V}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The electric field associated with a light wave at a point is given by $E = (100 \text{ Vm}^{-1}) \sin\!\left[(3.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ s}^{-1}) t\right] \sin\!\left[(6.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ s}^{-1}) t\right]$. When this light is incident on a metal surface with a work function of $2.0 \text{ eV}$, determine the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons.
questions.solution:
The given electric field of the light wave is:

$E = 100 \sin(3.0 \times 10^{15} t) \sin(6.0 \times 10^{15} t)$

Using the trigonometric identity $2 \sin A \sin B = \cos(A-B) - \cos(A+B)$, the electric field can be written as:

$E = 50 [\cos(3.0 \times 10^{15} t) - \cos(9.0 \times 10^{15} t)]$

This indicates that the light wave is a superposition of two waves with angular frequencies $\omega_1 = 3.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ s}^{-1}$ and $\omega_2 = 9.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ s}^{-1}$.

The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is determined by the photon with the higher frequency, which is $\omega_2 = 9.0 \times 10^{15} \text{ s}^{-1}$.

The energy of this incident photon is:

$E_p = \dfrac{h \omega_2}{2\pi} = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 9.0 \times 10^{15}}{2 \pi \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV}$

$E_p \approx \dfrac{59.67 \times 10^{-19}}{10.05 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV} \approx 5.93 \text{ eV}$

Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy is:

$K_{\text{max}} = E_p - \phi$

$K_{\text{max}} = 5.93 \text{ eV} - 2.0 \text{ eV} = 3.93 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: Determine the maximum magnitude of the linear momentum of a photoelectron emitted when light of wavelength $400 \text{ nm}$ is incident on a metal having a work function of $2.5 \text{ eV}$.
questions.solution:
The energy of the incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{400 \times 10^{-9}} \text{ J} = 4.97 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

The work function of the metal in Joules is:

$\Phi = 2.5 \text{ eV} = 2.5 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 4.0 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectron is:

$K_{\text{max}} = E - \Phi = 4.97 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} - 4.0 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 0.97 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

The maximum magnitude of the linear momentum is related to the maximum kinetic energy by:

$p_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{2 m_e K_{\text{max}}}$

Substituting the mass of an electron $m_e = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$:

$p_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.97 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ kg m/s}$

$p_{\text{max}} = \sqrt{17.65 \times 10^{-50}} \text{ kg m/s} \approx 4.2 \times 10^{-25} \text{ kg m/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The figure displays the plot of the stopping potential versus the frequency of the light used in an experiment on the photoelectric effect. Determine the ratio $h/e$.
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation,

$eV = h u - \Phi$

$V = \left(\dfrac{h}{e} ight) u - \dfrac{\Phi}{e}$

The graph of stopping potential $V$ versus frequency $ u$ is a straight line with slope equal to $\dfrac{h}{e}$.

From the given graph, we can take two points: $( u_1, V_1) = (1 imes 10^{14} ext{ Hz}, 0 ext{ V})$ and $( u_2, V_2) = (5 imes 10^{14} ext{ Hz}, 1.656 ext{ V})$.

Slope $= \dfrac{V_2 - V_1}{ u_2 - u_1}$

$\dfrac{h}{e} = \dfrac{1.656 - 0}{(5 - 1) imes 10^{14}}$

$\dfrac{h}{e} = \dfrac{1.656}{4 imes 10^{14}}$

$\dfrac{h}{e} = 0.414 imes 10^{-14} ext{ V s}$

$\dfrac{h}{e} = 4.14 imes 10^{-15} ext{ V s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q:
In a photoelectric effect experiment, the stopping potential is measured for monochromatic light beams of varying wavelengths. Determine the work function of the emitter from the experimental data.
wavelength (nm)$350$$400$$450$$500$$550$
stopping potential (V)$1.45$$1.00$$0.66$$0.38$$0.16$
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$eV_s = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} - \phi$

where $V_s$ is the stopping potential, $\lambda$ is the wavelength of incident light, and $\phi$ is the work function of the material.

Using the standard value $hc \approx 1240 \text{ eV nm}$, we can calculate the work function for the given data points.

For $\lambda = 400 \text{ nm}$ and $V_s = 1.00 \text{ V}$:

$\phi = \dfrac{1240}{400} - 1.00$

$\phi = 3.10 - 1.00 = 2.10 \text{ eV}$

For $\lambda = 500 \text{ nm}$ and $V_s = 0.38 \text{ V}$:

$\phi = \dfrac{1240}{500} - 0.38$

$\phi = 2.48 - 0.38 = 2.10 \text{ eV}$

The calculated work function is approximately $2.10 \text{ eV}$. Among the given choices, the closest value is $2.15 \text{ eV}$.

Answer: $2.15 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: The plot of the stopping potential versus the frequency of the light used in an experiment on the photoelectric effect is a straight line. The stopping potential is $0 \text{ V}$ at a frequency of $1 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$ and $1.656 \text{ V}$ at a frequency of $5 \times 10^{14} \text{ Hz}$. Determine the work function.
questions.solution:
According to Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$eV = h u - \Phi$

From the given graph, the stopping potential is $0 ext{ V}$ at the threshold frequency $ u_0 = 1 imes 10^{14} ext{ Hz}$.

Thus, the work function is $\Phi = h u_0 = h(1 imes 10^{14})$.

At $ u = 5 imes 10^{14} ext{ Hz}$, the stopping potential is $V = 1.656 ext{ V}$. Substituting these values into the equation:

$e(1.656) = h(5 imes 10^{14}) - \Phi$

Substituting $h(1 imes 10^{14}) = \Phi$ into the above equation:

$1.656 ext{ eV} = 5\Phi - \Phi$

$4\Phi = 1.656 ext{ eV}$

$\Phi = \dfrac{1.656}{4} ext{ eV} = 0.414 ext{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A photographic film is coated with a layer of silver bromide. When light strikes this film, the silver bromide molecules dissociate, and the film records the light. A minimum of $0.6 \text{ eV}$ is required to dissociate a silver bromide molecule. Determine the maximum wavelength of light that can be recorded by the film.
questions.solution:
The minimum energy required to dissociate a silver bromide molecule is given as $E = 0.6 \text{ eV}$.

The energy of a photon is related to its wavelength by the equation $E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$.

For maximum wavelength, the energy of the photon must be equal to the minimum dissociation energy.

$\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{hc}{E_{min}}$

Substituting $hc = 1242 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}$ and $E_{min} = 0.6 \text{ eV}$:

$\lambda_{max} = \dfrac{1242}{0.6} \text{ nm}$

$\lambda_{max} = 2070 \text{ nm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During an experiment on the photoelectric effect, light of wavelength $400 \text{ nm}$ illuminates a cesium plate at a rate of $5.0 \text{ W}$. The potential of the collector plate is kept sufficiently positive relative to the emitter to ensure the current attains its saturation value. Given that, on average, one out of every $10^6$ incident photons succeeds in ejecting a photoelectron, determine the photocurrent in the circuit.
questions.solution:
The energy of a single incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

Substituting the given values:

$E = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{400 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 4.97 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

The number of photons incident on the plate per second is:

$N = \dfrac{P}{E} = \dfrac{5.0}{4.97 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 1.0 \times 10^{19} \text{ s}^{-1}$

Since one out of every $10^6$ photons ejects a photoelectron, the number of photoelectrons emitted per second is:

$n = N \times 10^{-6} = 1.0 \times 10^{19} \times 10^{-6} = 1.0 \times 10^{13} \text{ s}^{-1}$

The saturation photocurrent is the total charge of these electrons per unit time:

$I = ne = 1.0 \times 10^{13} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} = 1.6 \times 10^{-6} \text{ A} = 1.6 \text{ \mu A}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: In a photoelectric effect experiment, the emitter and collector plates are separated by $10 \text{ cm}$ and connected via an ammeter without any cell (as shown in the figure). A magnetic field $B$ is applied parallel to the plates. The emitter has a work function of $2.39 \text{ eV}$, and the incident light contains wavelengths ranging from $400 \text{ nm}$ to $600 \text{ nm}$. Determine the minimum value of $B$ such that the current registered by the ammeter becomes zero. Neglect any space charge effects.
questions.solution:
The maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons corresponds to the minimum wavelength of the incident light, $\lambda = 400 \text{ nm}$.

The energy of the incident photon is given by:
$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1242 \text{ eV nm}}{400 \text{ nm}} = 3.105 \text{ eV}$

Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy $K_{\max}$ is:
$K_{\max} = E - \phi = 3.105 \text{ eV} - 2.39 \text{ eV} = 0.715 \text{ eV}$

For the ammeter to register zero current, the magnetic field must be strong enough to prevent even the most energetic photoelectrons from reaching the collector plate. Assuming the limiting case is for electrons emitted perpendicular to the plates, their trajectory will be a circle. To just miss the opposite plate, the radius of this circular path must be equal to the plate separation $d$.

$R = \dfrac{mv_{\max}}{eB} = d$

The maximum velocity $v_{\max}$ is related to $K_{\max}$ by $K_{\max} = \dfrac{1}{2}mv_{\max}^2$, which gives $v_{\max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2K_{\max}}{m}}$.

Substituting $v_{\max}$ into the radius equation:
$B = \dfrac{\sqrt{2mK_{\max}}}{ed}$

Substituting the standard values ($m = 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \text{ kg}$, $e = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ C}$, $d = 0.1 \text{ m}$):
$B = \dfrac{\sqrt{2 \times 9.1 \times 10^{-31} \times 0.715 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 0.1}$

$B = \dfrac{\sqrt{20.82 \times 10^{-50}}}{1.6 \times 10^{-20}}$

$B = \dfrac{4.56 \times 10^{-25}}{1.6 \times 10^{-20}} = 2.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

Answer: $2.85 \times 10^{-5} \text{ T}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A silver ball of radius $4.8 \text{ cm}$ is suspended by a thread within a vacuum chamber. Ultraviolet light having a wavelength of $200 \text{ nm}$ strikes the ball for a duration such that a total light energy of $1.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ J}$ is received on the surface. If, on average, one photon out of every ten thousand successfully ejects a photoelectron, determine the electric potential at the surface of the ball (taking the potential at infinity as zero) and the potential at the centre of the ball.
options: $0.3 \text{ V}$ at the surface, $0.3 \text{ V}$ at the centre
$3.0 \text{ V}$ at the surface, $3.0 \text{ V}$ at the centre
$3.0 \text{ V}$ at the surface, $0 \text{ V}$ at the centre
$0.3 \text{ V}$ at the surface, $0 \text{ V}$ at the centre
questions.solution:
The energy of a single incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{6.63 \times 10^{-34} \times 3 \times 10^8}{200 \times 10^{-9}} = 9.945 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

The total number of photons striking the surface is:

$n = \dfrac{E_{\text{total}}}{E} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-7}}{9.945 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 1.005 \times 10^{11}$

Given that one out of every $10^4$ photons ejects a photoelectron, the total number of ejected electrons is:

$n_e = n \times 10^{-4} = 1.005 \times 10^{11} \times 10^{-4} = 1.005 \times 10^7$

The total positive charge developed on the silver ball due to the emission of electrons is:

$q = n_e \times e = 1.005 \times 10^7 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \approx 1.608 \times 10^{-12} \text{ C}$

The electric potential at the surface of the spherical ball is:

$V_{\text{surface}} = \dfrac{1}{4\pi\epsilon_0} \dfrac{q}{R} = \dfrac{9 \times 10^9 \times 1.608 \times 10^{-12}}{0.048} \approx 0.3 \text{ V}$

Since silver is a conducting material, the electric field inside the ball is zero, which means the electric potential is constant throughout its volume. Therefore, the potential at the centre of the ball is equal to the potential at its surface:

$V_{\text{centre}} = V_{\text{surface}} = 0.3 \text{ V}$

Answer: $0.3 \text{ V}$ at the surface, $0.3 \text{ V}$ at the centre

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: For the setup illustrated in the figure, $y = 1.0 \text{ mm}$, $d = 0.24 \text{ mm}$, and $D = 1.2 \text{ m}$. The work function of the material of the emitter is $2.2 \text{ eV}$. Determine the stopping potential $V$ required to halt the photocurrent.
questions.solution:
From the given figure, the distance from the central bright fringe to the first dark fringe in the interference pattern is $y$.

The position of the first dark fringe is given by:

$y = \dfrac{\lambda D}{2d}$

Rearranging for the wavelength $\lambda$ of the light source:

$\lambda = \dfrac{2yd}{D}$

Substituting the given values $y = 1.0 \text{ mm} = 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, $d = 0.24 \text{ mm} = 0.24 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$, and $D = 1.2 \text{ m}$:

$\lambda = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.24 \times 10^{-3}}{1.2} = 0.4 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} = 400 \text{ nm}$

The energy of the incident photons is:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV nm}}{400 \text{ nm}} = 3.1 \text{ eV}$

Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, the stopping potential $V$ is given by:

$eV = E - \phi$

Substituting $E = 3.1 \text{ eV}$ and the work function $\phi = 2.2 \text{ eV}$:

$eV = 3.1 \text{ eV} - 2.2 \text{ eV} = 0.9 \text{ eV}$

Therefore, the stopping potential is $V = 0.9 \text{ V}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A monochromatic light source with an intensity of $5 \text{ mW}$ emits $8 \times 10^{15}$ photons per second. This light ejects photoelectrons from a metal surface. The stopping potential for this arrangement is $2.0 \text{ V}$. Determine the work function of the metal.
questions.solution:
The energy of a single photon is given by $E = \dfrac{P}{n}$

Substituting the given values:

$E = \dfrac{5 \times 10^{-3}}{8 \times 10^{15}} = 6.25 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

Converting the energy into electron-volts:

$E = \dfrac{6.25 \times 10^{-19}}{1.6 \times 10^{-19}} \text{ eV} \approx 3.9 \text{ eV}$

From Einstein's photoelectric equation:

$E = \Phi + K_{\max}$

Since the stopping potential is $V_s = 2.0 \text{ V}$, the maximum kinetic energy is $K_{\max} = 2.0 \text{ eV}$.

$\Phi = E - K_{\max} = 3.9 \text{ eV} - 2.0 \text{ eV} = 1.9 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: During a photoelectric experiment, the collector plate is maintained at $2.0 \text{ V}$ relative to a copper emitter plate ($\phi = 4.5 \text{ eV}$). A monochromatic light source of wavelength $200 \text{ nm}$ illuminates the emitter. Determine the minimum and maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons upon reaching the collector.
questions.solution:
The energy of the incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV nm}}{200 \text{ nm}} = 6.2 \text{ eV}$

According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is:

$K_{\max} = E - \phi = 6.2 \text{ eV} - 4.5 \text{ eV} = 1.7 \text{ eV}$

The minimum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is:

$K_{\min} = 0 \text{ eV}$

The collector plate is maintained at a positive potential of $2.0 \text{ V}$ relative to the emitter. Therefore, the electrons are accelerated and gain an additional kinetic energy of $2.0 \text{ eV}$.

The maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons upon reaching the collector is:

$K'_{\max} = K_{\max} + eV = 1.7 \text{ eV} + 2.0 \text{ eV} = 3.7 \text{ eV}$

The minimum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons upon reaching the collector is:

$K'_{\min} = K_{\min} + eV = 0 \text{ eV} + 2.0 \text{ eV} = 2.0 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small piece of cesium metal ($\phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$) is positioned $20 \text{ cm}$ away from a large metal plate, which has a charge density of $1.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C m}^{-2}$ on the surface facing the cesium. Monochromatic light with a wavelength of $400 \text{ nm}$ is incident on the cesium piece. Determine the minimum and maximum kinetic energy of the photoelectrons upon reaching the large metal plate. Neglect any change in the electric field due to the presence of the small piece of cesium.
questions.solution:
The energy of the incident photons is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV nm}}{400 \text{ nm}} = 3.1 \text{ eV}$

According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is:

$K_{\max} = E - \phi = 3.1 \text{ eV} - 1.9 \text{ eV} = 1.2 \text{ eV}$

The minimum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons is:

$K_{\min} = 0 \text{ eV}$

For a large metal plate, the electric field just outside the surface with a local charge density $\sigma$ is given by Gauss's law as:

$E_f = \dfrac{\sigma}{\epsilon_0} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-9}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 113 \text{ V/m}$

The potential difference between the large metal plate and the cesium piece at a distance $d = 20 \text{ cm} = 0.2 \text{ m}$ is:

$\Delta V = E_f \times d = 113 \times 0.2 = 22.6 \text{ V}$

Since the electric field points away from the positively charged plate, it does positive work on the negatively charged electrons moving towards it. The work done on each photoelectron is:

$W = e \Delta V = 22.6 \text{ eV}$

The kinetic energy of the photoelectrons upon reaching the plate will increase by this amount. Therefore, the minimum and maximum kinetic energies at the plate are:

$K'_{\min} = K_{\min} + W = 0 \text{ eV} + 22.6 \text{ eV} = 22.6 \text{ eV}$

$K'_{\max} = K_{\max} + W = 1.2 \text{ eV} + 22.6 \text{ eV} = 23.8 \text{ eV}$

Answer: $22.6 \text{ eV}$ and $23.8 \text{ eV}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A small piece of cesium metal ($\phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$) is positioned $20 \text{ cm}$ away from a large metal plate. The surface of the plate facing the cesium piece carries a charge density of $1.0 \times 10^{-9} \text{ C m}^{-2}$. Monochromatic light with a wavelength of $400 \text{ nm}$ illuminates the cesium piece. Considering the fastest electron emitted parallel to the large metal plate, determine its displacement parallel to its initial velocity right before it hits the plate. Assume the electric field remains unaffected by the presence of the small piece of cesium.
questions.solution:
The energy of the incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV nm}}{400 \text{ nm}} = 3.1 \text{ eV}$

Using Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted electron is:

$K_{\max} = E - \phi = 3.1 \text{ eV} - 1.9 \text{ eV} = 1.2 \text{ eV}$

The fastest electron emitted parallel to the plate has its entire kinetic energy in the direction parallel to the plate. Its initial velocity $v$ is:

$v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K_{\max}}{m}}$

For a large metal plate, the electric field just outside the surface carrying a local surface charge density $\sigma$ is given by Gauss's law as:

$E_{\text{field}} = \dfrac{\sigma}{\varepsilon_0} = \dfrac{1.0 \times 10^{-9}}{8.85 \times 10^{-12}} \approx 113 \text{ V/m}$

The electric force on the electron is perpendicular to its initial velocity, causing an acceleration towards the plate:

$a = \dfrac{e E_{\text{field}}}{m}$

The time $t$ taken for the electron to travel the distance $d = 0.2 \text{ m}$ to the plate is:

$d = \dfrac{1}{2} a t^2 \Rightarrow t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2d}{a}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2d m}{e E_{\text{field}}}}$

The displacement $x$ parallel to the initial velocity right before it hits the plate is:

$x = v t = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K_{\max}}{m}} \sqrt{\dfrac{2d m}{e E_{\text{field}}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 d K_{\max}}{e E_{\text{field}}}}$

Substituting the values (note that $K_{\max} = 1.2 \text{ e}$ Joules):

$x = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 0.2 \text{ m} \times 1.2 \text{ eV}}{e \times 113 \text{ V/m}}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{0.96}{113} \text{ m}^2}$

$x = \sqrt{0.008496} \text{ m} \approx 0.092 \text{ m} = 9.2 \text{ cm}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A horizontal cesium plate ($\phi = 1.9 \text{ eV}$) travels vertically downward with a steady speed $v$ inside a room filled with radiation of wavelength $250 \text{ nm}$ and greater. Determine the minimum value of $v$ such that the vertically upward velocity component remains nonpositive for every photoelectron.
questions.solution:
The maximum energy of the incident photons corresponds to the minimum wavelength $\lambda = 250 \text{ nm}$.

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda} = \dfrac{1240 \text{ eV nm}}{250 \text{ nm}} = 4.96 \text{ eV}$

According to Einstein's photoelectric equation, the maximum kinetic energy of the emitted photoelectrons relative to the plate is:

$K_{max} = E - \phi = 4.96 \text{ eV} - 1.9 \text{ eV} = 3.06 \text{ eV}$

$K_{max} = 3.06 \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J} = 4.896 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}$

The maximum velocity of the photoelectrons relative to the plate is:

$u_{max} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 K_{max}}{m_e}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \times 4.896 \times 10^{-19}}{9.1 \times 10^{-31}}} \approx 1.04 \times 10^6 \text{ m/s}$

The velocity of a photoelectron relative to the ground is $\vec{v}_{e} = \vec{u} + \vec{v}_{plate}$, where $\vec{v}_{plate}$ is directed vertically downward with speed $v$.

For the vertically upward velocity component of every photoelectron to remain nonpositive, the downward speed of the plate $v$ must be greater than or equal to the maximum possible upward velocity of the photoelectron relative to the plate.

$v \ge u_{max} \Rightarrow v_{min} = 1.04 \times 10^6 \text{ m/s}$

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal plate with a work function of $2.2 \text{ eV}$ is illuminated by a light beam having a wavelength of $400 \text{ nm}$. A particular electron absorbs a photon and undergoes two collisions before emerging from the metal. Assuming that $10\%$ of the extra energy is lost to the metal during each collision, determine the kinetic energy of this electron as it exits the metal.
questions.solution:
Energy of the incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

Using $hc = 1240 \text{ eV}\cdot\text{nm}$ and $\lambda = 400 \text{ nm}$:

$E = \dfrac{1240}{400} = 3.1 \text{ eV}$

The electron absorbs this photon, so its extra energy inside the metal is $3.1 \text{ eV}$.

During each collision, the electron loses $10\%$ of its extra energy to the metal, meaning it retains $90\%$ of its energy after each collision.

Energy remaining after two collisions is:

$E' = 3.1 \times (0.9)^2 = 3.1 \times 0.81 = 2.511 \text{ eV}$

To emerge from the metal, the electron must overcome the work function $\Phi = 2.2 \text{ eV}$.

The kinetic energy of the electron as it exits the metal is:

$K = E' - \Phi$

$K = 2.511 - 2.2 = 0.311 \text{ eV}$

Rounding to two decimal places, the kinetic energy is $0.31 \text{ eV}$.

questions.subject: Physics
questions.q: A metal plate with a work function of $2.2 \text{ eV}$ is illuminated by a light beam having a wavelength of $400 \text{ nm}$. A particular electron absorbs a photon. Assuming that $10\%$ of the extra energy is lost to the metal during each collision, determine the maximum number of collisions the electron can undergo before it becomes unable to emerge from the metal.
questions.solution:
The energy of the incident photon is given by:

$E = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$

Substituting $hc = 1240 \text{ eV nm}$ and $\lambda = 400 \text{ nm}$:

$E = \dfrac{1240}{400} = 3.1 \text{ eV}$

When the electron absorbs the photon, it gains an extra energy of $3.1 \text{ eV}$. During each collision, the electron loses $10\%$ of its energy, meaning it retains $90\%$ of its energy.

After $n$ collisions, the remaining energy of the electron is:

$E_n = E \times (0.9)^n$

For the electron to emerge from the metal, its remaining energy must be greater than the work function of the metal ($\phi = 2.2 \text{ eV}$). Thus, we require:

$E_n > 2.2 \text{ eV}$

Calculating the remaining energy after successive collisions:
After $1$ collision: $E_1 = 3.1 \times 0.9 = 2.79 \text{ eV}$
After $2$ collisions: $E_2 = 2.79 \times 0.9 = 2.511 \text{ eV}$
After $3$ collisions: $E_3 = 2.511 \times 0.9 = 2.2599 \text{ eV}$
After $4$ collisions: $E_4 = 2.2599 \times 0.9 \approx 2.034 \text{ eV}$

Since $E_3 > 2.2 \text{ eV}$ and $E_4 < 2.2 \text{ eV}$, the electron can undergo a maximum of $3$ collisions and still have enough energy to emerge from the metal.

Answer: $3$
